title: The Unit Impulse Function δ(t)
summary: This page introduces the unit impulse function δ(t), defined by Dirac as zero everywhere except at t=0 with unit area under the curve. It explains the impulse as a limiting case of a tall, narrow rectangular pulse with infinitesimally small width and infinitely large height but unit area. The page also includes Drill 1.8, which demonstrates how a piecewise signal can be expressed using unit step functions.

figures.caption: A piecewise signal x(t) that ramps linearly from 0 to 1 between t=1 and t=2, remains at 1 from t=2 to t=4, and is zero elsewhere.
figures.description: The figure is a plot of the signal x(t) versus time t, with the vertical axis labeled x(t) and the horizontal axis labeled t. The waveform is zero before t=1, rises linearly from 0 to 1 between t=1 and t=2, stays constant at amplitude 1 from t=2 to t=4, and then drops vertically back to 0 at t=4. Students should notice the breakpoints at t=1, 2, and 4, the ramp segment with slope 1, and the flat plateau at x(t)=1 indicated by the dashed guide lines.

figures.caption: The Dirac delta function δ(t) shown symbolically in (a) and represented in (b) as the limit of a rectangular pulse of width ε and height 1/ε as ε→0, illustrating one common approximation of the impulse function.
figures.description: The figure has two time-axis plots labeled (a) and (b), both with horizontal axis t and the origin marked at 0. In (a), the Dirac delta is shown symbolically as an upward arrow at t = 0 labeled δ(t). In (b), a rectangular pulse centered at t = 0 extends from -ε/2 to ε/2 with height 1/ε, and an arrow indicates ε → 0. Students should notice that as the pulse width shrinks and the height grows, it represents an impulse concentrated at t = 0 while maintaining constant unit area.
